searchSchema.description: The typed search fields as a JSON Schema (draft 2020-12) object, serialized to a JSON string.

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You build search templates for vendor websites (retail, cars, real estate, etc.).
  A template has three parts that you persist together: a typed search schema, an HTTP
  request mapping, and a result mapping.
  
  Workflow:
  1. Call analyze-provider with the Provider's ref to fetch the vendor site's page source.
  2. Inspect the source to understand how the site exposes search and lists results.
  3. Derive the search schema as a JSONSchema object whose properties are the typed search
     fields the user can fill in (string / number / boolean). Express ranges (e.g. min/max
     price, year) as separate number properties annotated accordingly.
  4. Derive the request mapping: prefer a formal API endpoint if one is discoverable;
     otherwise use the site's search URL with query parameters. Bind each request parameter
     to a search-schema field (use the 'min' / 'max' transform hints for range fields).
  5. Derive the result mapping: choose responseType ('html' or 'json'), an itemLocator that
     selects each listing, and per-field extractors for title, url, price, and image
     (CSS selector + attribute for html, JSONPath for json). The page source begins with a
     "Repeating elements" summary listing candidate container selectors with their frequency;
     use the top candidate (the element that wraps the fields and repeats once per result) as
     the itemLocator, and prefer the exact selector shown — e.g. a "^=" prefix selector when
     container ids are indexed (advertCard-0, advertCard-1), not an exact match.
  6. Call set-provider-template with the Provider's ref, the request and result mappings, and
     the searchSchema as a JSON string (a JSON Schema object serialized with JSON.stringify,
     e.g. '{"type":"object","properties":{"make":{"type":"string","title":"Make"}}}').
  
  Be conservative: only include fields and selectors you can justify from the page source.
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

<object>
  <dxn>echo://BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U/01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNT89</dxn>
  <typename>org.dxos.type.commerce.Provider</typename>
</object>
Analyze the AutoTrader provider in your context and generate and persist its search template (search schema, request mapping, result mapping) by calling analyzeProvider then setProviderTemplate.

Now I have a thorough understanding of the AutoTrader UK page. Let me analyze the key parts: **URL pattern** from the skip-to-content link: `https://www.autotrader.co.uk/car-search?channel=cars&postcode=ws149el&make=Porsche&model=911&homeDeliveryAdverts=include&advertising-location=at_cars&year-to=2026` **Search parameters visible:** - `make` - e.g., "Porsche" - `model` - e.g., "911" - `postcode` - e.g., "ws149el" - `year-to` - e.g., 2026 (max year) - `channel` - always "cars" - `advertising-location` - always "at_cars" - `homeDeliveryAdverts` - always "include" From the filter pills, I can see the additional filters available like price ranges, mileage, gearbox type, body type, fuel type, engine size, distance radius, and year ranges. The URL parameters I'd need to construct would include make, model, postcode, year-from and year-to, price ranges, and likely similar parameter names for the other filters. For scraping the listings, the best selector is the `[data-advertid]` attribute on the `
  • ` elements since each one wraps around 17 fields, though the `[data-testid^="advertCard-"]` div inside also repeats 24 times. The title field uses `[data-testid="search-listing-title"]` to get the text content like "Porsche". For the URL, I'm pulling the `href` attribute from that same title selector. The price is trickier—it's in a span with hashed class names like `sc-1mc7cl3-14` that could change, and there's no data-testid attribute to rely on, so I need to figure out a more stable selector approach. I don't have a reliable data-testid for the price, so I'm considering using the hidden accessibility span within the title or targeting the visible price span through its class selectors, though neither approach is ideal since they lack explicit test identifiers. For the carousel images, there's no img tag in the HTML structure I can see, likely because they load lazily, so I can't reliably select them that way. For the item locator itself, I'll use the advertCard data-testid prefix since it wraps all the listing content and repeats for each item. Between the two candidates that both appear 24 times—the outer `[data-advertid]` list item and the inner `[data-testid^="advertCard-"]` div—I should go with the top candidate, which is `[data-advertid]`. For the search schema, I'm mapping the URL parameters and filter pills to their field names: make, model, postcode, and the year/price ranges with their hyphenated versions. Now I need to set up the request configuration with GET method, the base URL, and the fixed query parameters like channel and advertising-location, then map the variable parameters from the schema. For extracting results, I'm using the HTML response type with `[data-advertid]` as the item locator, and I've got selectors for title and URL from the data-testid attributes, but I still need to find the right selector for the price field since the visible price span doesn't have a data-testid to target. The hidden span inside the title contains both subtitle and price text mixed together, which makes extraction messy. I notice the subtitle and attention grabber have their own data-testids, and mileage and year do too, but price doesn't. The hidden span class appears elsewhere on the page too, so it's not a reliable selector. I think I need to reconsider the approach rather than trying to parse combined text from the title element. the advertCard structure shows the price is nested several levels deep within styled-component divs, and while the class `sc-1mc7cl3-14` appears consistent, it's fragile since styled-component classes can change between builds. Since there's no data-testid for the price element, I'll go with extracting it from the hidden span in the title, which contains the price alongside the subtitle text. Now I'm mapping out the available selectors: I can use `[data-testid="search-listing-title"]` for both the title text and URL, `[data-testid="search-listing-subtitle"]` for the subtitle, and parse the price from the hidden span text which follows a consistent format with the price appearing after a comma at the end. Writing schema JSON... Writing the response mapping... I'm realizing the price selector is pulling from a span inside the title element, which includes extra text like the model name and full price string. I need to find a more specific selector that isolates just the price value itself. I should stick to what I can actually justify from the page source. The `[data-testid="search-listing-title"] span` selector is solid since it's anchored by the data-testid attribute, and there's only one span inside the title link containing the price text that I can parse out. The hidden span does double duty—it includes both subtitle and price for accessibility—but since the subtitle has its own selector via `[data-testid="search-listing-subtitle"]`, I can extract the price from the span text reliably. For the core fields, I'll focus on title, url, price, and image as the blueprint suggests, then add mileage, year, and location since they have clear selectors. Since the images appear to be lazy-loaded through CSS or JavaScript rather than actual `` tags in the source, I'll skip that field. Let me finalize the selector strategy and build out the fields object. Looking more closely at the price span structure, the hash-generated classes like `gmXvZp` and `gNFmcp` are consistent across listings but fragile. I'll stick with extracting the price from the accessible span text since it's more reliable than relying on these generated class names.

I've analyzed the AutoTrader UK page source. Now I have a clear picture of the URL structure, search parameters, repeating listing containers, and the data-testid selectors for each field. Let me persist the template.

**Key findings:**
- **URL pattern**: `https://www.autotrader.co.uk/car-search` with query params like `make`, `model`, `postcode`, `year-from`, `year-to`, `price-from`, `price-to`, `maximum-mileage`
- **Item container**: `[data-advertid]` (×24, wraps ~17 fields) — the top repeating candidate
- **Field selectors**: `[data-testid="search-listing-title"]` (title/url), `[data-testid="search-listing-subtitle"]` (subtitle), `[data-testid="mileage"]`, `[data-testid="registered_year"]`, `[data-testid="search-listing-location"]`
- **Price**: Extracted from the visually-hidden `
` tags in the static HTML, so omitted.

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You build search templates for vendor websites (retail, cars, real estate, etc.).
  A template has three parts that you persist together: a typed search schema, an HTTP
  request mapping, and a result mapping.
  
  Workflow:
  1. Call analyze-provider with the Provider's ref to fetch the vendor site's page source.
  2. Inspect the source to understand how the site exposes search and lists results.
  3. Derive the search schema as a JSONSchema object whose properties are the typed search
     fields the user can fill in (string / number / boolean). Express ranges (e.g. min/max
     price, year) as separate number properties annotated accordingly.
  4. Derive the request mapping: prefer a formal API endpoint if one is discoverable;
     otherwise use the site's search URL with query parameters. Bind each request parameter
     to a search-schema field (use the 'min' / 'max' transform hints for range fields).
  5. Derive the result mapping: choose responseType ('html' or 'json'), an itemLocator that
     selects each listing, and per-field extractors for title, url, price, and image
     (CSS selector + attribute for html, JSONPath for json). The page source begins with a
     "Repeating elements" summary listing candidate container selectors with their frequency;
     use the top candidate (the element that wraps the fields and repeats once per result) as
     the itemLocator, and prefer the exact selector shown — e.g. a "^=" prefix selector when
     container ids are indexed (advertCard-0, advertCard-1), not an exact match.
  6. Call set-provider-template with the Provider's ref, the request and result mappings, and
     the searchSchema as a JSON string (a JSON Schema object serialized with JSON.stringify,
     e.g. '{"type":"object","properties":{"make":{"type":"string","title":"Make"}}}').
  
  Be conservative: only include fields and selectors you can justify from the page source.
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

<object>
  <dxn>echo://BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U/01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNT89</dxn>
  <typename>org.dxos.type.commerce.Provider</typename>
</object>
Analyze the AutoTrader provider in your context and generate and persist its search template (search schema, request mapping, result mapping) by calling analyzeProvider then setProviderTemplate.

tools.name: analyze-provider
tools.description: Fetches the provider's vendor site and returns the page source for the agent to inspect.

provider.description:
The URI of the referenced object. Protocols accepted:
- echo://01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4. 
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)
- dxn:org.dxos.type.calendar:0.1.0 -- named entity from the registry. Absoutely make sure it actually exists in the registry.

URIs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.

tools.name: set-provider-template
tools.description: Persists the derived search schema, request mapping, and result mapping onto a provider.

provider.description:
The URI of the referenced object. Protocols accepted:
- echo://01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4. 
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

searchSchema.description: The typed search fields as a JSON Schema (draft 2020-12) object, serialized to a JSON string.

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You build search templates for vendor websites (retail, cars, real estate, etc.).
  A template has three parts that you persist together: a typed search schema, an HTTP
  request mapping, and a result mapping.
  
  Workflow:
  1. Call analyze-provider with the Provider's ref to fetch the vendor site's page source.
  2. Inspect the source to understand how the site exposes search and lists results.
  3. Derive the search schema as a JSONSchema object whose properties are the typed search
     fields the user can fill in (string / number / boolean). Express ranges (e.g. min/max
     price, year) as separate number properties annotated accordingly.
  4. Derive the request mapping: prefer a formal API endpoint if one is discoverable;
     otherwise use the site's search URL with query parameters. Bind each request parameter
     to a search-schema field (use the 'min' / 'max' transform hints for range fields).
  5. Derive the result mapping: choose responseType ('html' or 'json'), an itemLocator that
     selects each listing, and per-field extractors for title, url, price, and image
     (CSS selector + attribute for html, JSONPath for json). The page source begins with a
     "Repeating elements" summary listing candidate container selectors with their frequency;
     use the top candidate (the element that wraps the fields and repeats once per result) as
     the itemLocator, and prefer the exact selector shown — e.g. a "^=" prefix selector when
     container ids are indexed (advertCard-0, advertCard-1), not an exact match.
  6. Call set-provider-template with the Provider's ref, the request and result mappings, and
     the searchSchema as a JSON string (a JSON Schema object serialized with JSON.stringify,
     e.g. '{"type":"object","properties":{"make":{"type":"string","title":"Make"}}}').
  
  Be conservative: only include fields and selectors you can justify from the page source.
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

<object>
  <dxn>echo://BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U/01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNT89</dxn>
  <typename>org.dxos.type.commerce.Provider</typename>
</object>
Analyze the AutoTrader provider in your context and generate and persist its search template (search schema, request mapping, result mapping) by calling analyzeProvider then setProviderTemplate.
